title: Disorders of the Mediastinum
authors:
Stefi F. Lee, Rebecca M. Baron,

Hilary J. Goldberg

items.title: Anatomical Compartments of the Mediastinum and Associated Neoplasms
items.content: The mediastinum is divided into three compartments: anterior, middle, and posterior. Each compartment contains specific anatomical structures that can give rise to neoplasms.
keyPoints: Anterior mediastinum includes the thymus gland, anterior mediastinal lymph nodes, and internal mammary arteries and veins; it is a common site for several neoplasms.

Middle mediastinum contains the heart, major vessels like the ascending and transverse arches of the aorta, venae cavae, trachea, main bronchi, pulmonary arteries and veins, and their contiguous lymph nodes.

Posterior mediastinum extends from the posterior border of the heart and trachea to the vertebral column; it includes structures like the descending thoracic aorta, esophagus, thoracic duct, azygos and hemiazygos veins, and posterior group of mediastinal lymph nodes.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.title: Types of Disorders in the Mediastinum
items.content: The mediastinum can be affected by a variety of disorders, including neoplastic and nonneoplastic masses, congenital or acquired malformations, infections, and chronic fibrosing mediastinitis.
keyPoints: Disorders include neoplastic and nonneoplastic masses.
Conditions may involve congenital or acquired anatomical malformations.
Infections can affect the mediastinum.
Chronic fibrosing mediastinitis is a specific disorder addressed in this context.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.title: Anterior Mediastinum: Common Neoplasms and Diagnostic Markers
items.content: The anterior mediastinum is associated with specific neoplasms such as thymomas, germ cell tumors, teratomas, lymphomas, and thyroid goiter. Thymomas are notably linked to myasthenia gravis, diagnosed by serum anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies. Teratomas, a type of germ cell tumor, contain fat, soft tissue, and calcium, with elevated α-fetoprotein and β-human chorionic gonadotropin distinguishing between seminomatous and nonseminomatous types.
keyPoints: Thymomas are the most common neoplasm in the anterior mediastinum.
Myasthenia gravis is associated with thymomas, diagnosed by serum anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies.
Teratomas contain fat, soft tissue, and calcium.
Elevated α-fetoprotein and β-human chorionic gonadotropin distinguish between seminomatous and nonseminomatous germ cell tumors.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.title: Middle Mediastinum: Benign Masses and SVC Syndrome
items.content: The middle mediastinum primarily contains benign masses from embryonic abnormalities, such as bronchogenic cysts, duplication cysts, and pericardial cysts. However, malignancies can lead to life-threatening conditions like superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome due to compression of the SVC, often by lung cancer, particularly small-cell lung carcinoma.
keyPoints: Bronchogenic cysts arise from abnormal ventral foregut budding.
Duplication cysts result from persistent diverticulum of the dorsal bud of the foregut.
Pericardial cysts are due to abnormal fusion of pericardial recesses.
SVC syndrome presents with severe dyspnea and facial/upper extremity edema, often caused by lung cancer (up to 50% cases).
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.title: Posterior Mediastinum: Neurogenic Tumors and Diagnostic Imaging
items.content: Neurogenic tumors are the most common cause of posterior mediastinal masses, often benign. Other causes include diaphragmatic hernia and meningoceles. Barium studies are useful for diagnosing gastrointestinal-related lesions such as esophageal diverticula.
keyPoints: Neurogenic tumors are the most common cause of posterior mediastinal tumors.
Barium studies help diagnose hernias, diverticula, and achalasia in posterior mediastinal lesions.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.title: Acute Mediastinitis: Causes, Presentation, and Management
items.content: Acute mediastinitis is often caused by esophageal perforation or post-cardiac surgery complications. Patients present with chest pain, dyspnea, wound drainage, sepsis, or a widened mediastinum. Diagnosis is typically confirmed via mediastinal needle aspiration.
keyPoints: Causes include esophageal perforation, median sternotomy complications, and infections descending from the neck, oral cavity, or facial area.
Incidence of mediastinitis post-median sternotomy: 0.4–5.0%.
Treatment involves exploration with primary repair of the esophageal tear, drainage, debridement, and parenteral antibiotics.
Mortality rate exceeds 20% despite treatment.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.title: Chronic Mediastinitis: Pathophysiology and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Chronic mediastinitis ranges from granulomatous inflammation to fibrosing mediastinitis, often following infections like histoplasmosis or tuberculosis. Symptoms depend on the extent of invasion into mediastinal structures.
keyPoints: Causes include histoplasmosis, tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, silicosis, and fungal diseases.
Granulomatous mediastinitis patients are usually asymptomatic.
Fibrosing mediastinitis can cause dyspnea, chest pain, SVC syndrome, hemoptysis, dysphagia, and Horner's syndrome.
Diagnosis involves clinical evaluation, radiologic imaging (CT/MRI), and histopathologic findings.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.question: Which structure is NOT typically found in the anterior mediastinum?
items.explanation: The ascending aorta is located in the middle mediastinum, not the anterior mediastinum.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.question: Which compartment contains the esophagus?
items.explanation: The esophagus is located in the posterior mediastinum.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.question: What is a common cause of chronic fibrosing mediastinitis?
items.explanation: Chronic fibrosing mediastinitis is often caused by fungal infections, particularly histoplasmosis.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.question: Which neoplasm is most commonly associated with myasthenia gravis in the anterior mediastinum?
items.explanation: Thymomas are closely tied to paraneoplastic syndromes, most notably myasthenia gravis.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.question: What is the distinguishing feature of teratomas among germ cell tumors in the anterior mediastinum?
options: They contain fat, soft tissue, and calcium
They have elevated α fetoprotein levels
items.explanation: Teratomas are multilobulated cystic lesions that contain fat, soft tissue, and calcium.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.question: Which imaging technique is important for evaluating mediastinal masses due to its ability to attenuate various substances?
items.explanation: Computed tomography (CT) scanning can attenuate fat, water, calcifications, and air.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.question: Which mediastinal mass is most commonly associated with superior vena cava syndrome?
items.explanation: Lung cancer, particularly small-cell lung carcinoma, accounts for up to 50% of cases of SVC syndrome.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.question: Which of the following is a common cause of acute mediastinitis?
items.explanation: Acute mediastinitis is commonly due to esophageal perforation, among other causes.

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.question: What is the typical presentation of patients with mediastinitis after median sternotomy?
items.explanation: Patients most commonly present with wound drainage and sometimes sepsis after median sternotomy.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.question: What is the mortality rate for mediastinitis following a median sternotomy?
items.explanation: The mortality rate for mediastinitis after median sternotomy still exceeds 20%.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.statement: The middle mediastinum contains the heart and its major vessels.
items.explanation: The middle mediastinum includes the heart, ascending and transverse arches of the aorta, venae cavae, and other structures.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.statement: The posterior mediastinum is bounded anteriorly by the trachea.
items.explanation: The posterior mediastinum extends from the posterior border of the heart and trachea to the vertebral column.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · Disorders of the Mediastinum

items.statement: Surgical resection is the mainstay of treatment for lymphomas in the anterior mediastinum.
items.explanation: Lymphoma is treated with multimodal therapy, including radiation and chemotherapy; surgery is rarely indicated.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.statement: Neurogenic tumors are the most common cause of posterior mediastinal tumors.
items.explanation: Neurogenic tumors are indeed the most common cause of posterior mediastinal tumors.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.statement: Patients with granulomatous mediastinitis are usually symptomatic.
items.explanation: Patients with granulomatous mediastinitis are usually asymptomatic.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.subtopic: ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS
items.statement: Fibrosing mediastinitis can lead to obstruction of the superior vena cava (SVC).
items.explanation: Fibrosing mediastinitis can cause SVC syndrome due to obstruction.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · ACUTE MEDIASTINITIS | CHRONIC MEDIASTINITIS AND FIBROSING MEDIASTINITIS

items.assertion: Thymomas in the anterior mediastinum can be associated with paraneoplastic syndromes.
items.reason: The diagnosis of myasthenia gravis is supported by the presence of serum anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association with paraneoplastic syndromes.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES

items.assertion: Bronchogenic cysts are a common cause of superior vena cava syndrome.
items.reason: Bronchogenic cysts arise from abnormal ventral foregut budding in the middle mediastinum.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because bronchogenic cysts do not commonly cause SVC syndrome; however, the reason is true as they arise from abnormal ventral foregut budding.
items.reference: Disorders of the Mediastinum · MEDIASTINAL MASSES
